title: Interrupted Continuity: An Empirical Analysis of Interruptions in Continuous Pharma Manufacturing
text:
[span class=“hero-label”]{Working Paper · Operations Management · 2025}[/span]



Christopher Antwi · University of Oregon, Lundquist College of Business


Using approximately 300 million data points from a continuous tablet production line over 120 hours, this study demonstrates that unplanned exogenous interruptions propagate from the upstream feeding stage to the downstream compression stage, reducing productivity by 49%–96% during post-interruption transition periods while simultaneously compromising product quality through increased tablet weight and reduced drug content.

View Full Analysis Data Overview

title: Interrupted Continuity: An Empirical Analysis of Interruptions in Continuous Pharma Manufacturing
text:
Key Statistics



300M


Data points collected




120h


Production run duration




272


Exogenous interruptions




13,794


Observations (30s intervals)




$11,483


Est. annual material loss

title: Interrupted Continuity: An Empirical Analysis of Interruptions in Continuous Pharma Manufacturing
text:
Key Findings





Finding 1


Interruptions reduce productivity by up to 96% during post-interruption transition periods


Filling shoe speed — the primary proxy for downstream productivity — drops dramatically at interruption onset and recovers progressively over the subsequent 150 seconds, consistent with the system’s mean residence time of 136 seconds.




Finding 2


Upstream interruptions cascade downstream, affecting both productivity and quality


98.5% of identified interruptions originate at the feeding stage. These upstream stoppages propagate through blending to compression, reducing filling shoe speed and increasing the likelihood of weight control threshold violations.




Finding 3


Operators compensate by proactively increasing filling depth post-interruption


Following interruptions, operators increase volumetric filling depth at the tablet press — a conservative strategy that ensures individual tablets meet label claim requirements but generates excess material waste, estimated at $11,483 annually per production line.




Finding 4


OOS alarm systems show reduced sensitivity during post-interruption recovery


Blender 2 OOS pass rates paradoxically increase post-interruption, suggesting RTD-based potency predictions may fail to capture transient inhomogeneities during flow recovery — creating misleadingly favorable control signals.

title: Interrupted Continuity: An Empirical Analysis of Interruptions in Continuous Pharma Manufacturing

title: Interrupted Continuity: An Empirical Analysis of Interruptions in Continuous Pharma Manufacturing
text:
Process Overview



The GEA CDC-50 continuous direct compression system integrates six loss-in-weight feeders, two continuous blenders, and a rotary tablet press into a single automated workflow. Raw materials are continuously fed, blended, and compressed into core tablets — making the system highly efficient but also highly vulnerable to propagating disruptions.
PD1–PD5 (Feeders) → Blender 1 (B1) → PD7 (Lubricant) → Blender 2 (B2) → Tablet Press (KPP)
Data spans January 12–17, 2018, capturing 121 hours and 18 minutes of continuous operations. Machine data is recorded at one-second intervals; tablet quality data at approximately 15–30 minute intervals.
Full Data Description →

title: Interrupted Continuity: An Empirical Analysis of Interruptions in Continuous Pharma Manufacturing
text:
Citation

Antwi, C. (2025). Interrupted Continuity: An Empirical Analysis of Interruptions in Continuous Pharma Manufacturing. Working Paper, University of Oregon. Replication of Shao, Choudhary & Ding (2025), Nanyang Business School Research Paper No. 25-05.

title: Data
text:
This study uses data provided by MSD (Merck & Co., Inc.) in collaboration with GEA for the 2025 MSOM Data-Driven Research Challenge. The dataset captures approximately 300 million data points over five days of production from a CDC-50 continuous direct compression line.



The GEA CDC-50 system integrates six loss-in-weight (LiW) feeders, two continuous blenders, and a rotary tablet press. Raw materials flow continuously from feeders through blending to compression — with no intermediate hold times between stages.








Stage
Equipment
Role




Feeding
PD1–PD5 (LiW feeders)
Deliver controlled mass flow of individual components to Blender 1


Lubrication
PD7 (LiW feeder)
Adds magnesium stearate between B1 and B2


Blending
Blender 1 (B1)
High-shear homogenization; large holdup volume


Blending
Blender 2 (B2)
Low-shear lubrication mixing


Compression
KPP Tablet Press
Rotary compression into core tablets



Formulation (400 mg tablet):




Material


Identifier


Composition (%)


Quantity (mg)






Acetaminophen powder (API)


pAPAP (PD3)


10%


40




Spray dried lactose


L316 (PD2)


29%


116




Microcrystalline cellulose


A102 (PD1, PD4)


57%


228




Sodium croscarmellose


Ac-di-sol (PD5)


3%


12




Magnesium stearate


Mag St (PD7)


1%


4








Study period: January 12, 2018 08:42:31 — January 17, 2018 03:40:00 (94% of total production time)




Data type


Source


Frequency


Key variables






LiW Feeder machine data


Historian


1 second


Mass flow, screw RPM, feed factor, % composition (PD1–PD7)




Blender machine data


Historian


1 second


Mass flow B1/B2, blend potency predictions, OOS concentration




Tablet press machine data


Historian


1 second


Filling shoe speed (paddles 1 & 2), filling depth, compression force




Environmental data


Sensors


~15 min


Temperature and humidity at feeder and tablet press




Tablet quality (IPC)


Manual sampling


~15 min


Average weight (mg), thickness (mm), hardness (kP)




Content uniformity


At-line NIR


~30 min


Average drug content (%) — 10 tablets per sample




Event logbook


Operator notes


~30 min intervals


Process events, interruption causes, operator observations








Exogenous interruptions are identified in two steps:
Step 1 — Machine stoppages: 30-second slots where filling shoe speed equals zero, aggregated into contiguous runs. Runs exceeding 20 slots (10 minutes) are classified as planned maintenance pauses and excluded.
Step 2 — Logbook matching: Machine stop runs are matched to exogenous logbook entries within a ±30-minute window. Logbook entries classified as Feeder/Feeding, Electronic Communication, or Compression are retained as exogenous. RTD/PAT, NIR, Quality, Adjustment, Cleaning, and Blending entries are excluded.
This yields 272 exogenous stop slots across the study period, with 98.5% occurring during the feeding stage.

Interruption types identified:

Communication errors — feeder communication alarms resulting in automatic process stops
Material flow interruptions — vacuum wand voids, empty refills, powder buildup
Software glitches — loss of information causing system resets
Compression force anomalies — read/write differences triggering process stops










Variable


Description


Range






Interruption


Binary — exogenous stop at time t


0 / 1




Interruption.Feeding


Binary — exogenous feeding-stage stop


0 / 1




Speed


Filling shoe speed, paddle 1 (RPM)


0 – 80




Weight.Pass


Binary — weight deviation within ±1% of 400mg target


0 / 1




Weight


Average tablet weight (mg)


392.3 – 407.8




Drug.Content


Average API percentage per tablet (%)


9.8 – 10.4




Lot.Change


Binary — Avicel PH102 lot change period


0 / 1




Press.Temperature


Temperature at tablet press (°C)


23.6 – 39.6




Press.Humidity


Relative humidity at tablet press (%)


8.2 – 33.5












Parameter


Target


Control limit


Rejection limit






Tablet weight


400 mg


±1% (396–404 mg)


±4% (384–416 mg)




Drug content

±0.5% (9.5–10.5%)


±15% (8.5–11.5%)




RTD potency prediction


100%

2% OOS triggers rejection




%PD3 (API feeder)


10.0%


±15%


Outside 8.5–11.5%









Raw machine data is not publicly available due to data sharing agreements with MSD/GEA. Code, methodology, summary statistics, and analytical outputs are available in the GitHub repository.
GitHub Repository
